text: Yeah, let's kick this off.
Okay, so hello everyone.
I'm Leijie Wang.
I'm a PhD student from University of Washington.
Even though I'm at Seattle, very close to Vancouver,
but because of the visa issues, I cannot come in person.
So that's why I'm over the Zoom.
But today I would like to be glad to present our work about Blue Sky Custom Feeds
and the title, Understand the Landscape of Custom Feeds on Blue Sky.
I'll start with thinking about the platforms you use every day.
Like YouTube, TikTok, Instagram, or Spotify.
Each of them has a recommendation feed that feels quite personal to you.
But you have no saying how that particular algorithm works
or what you're optimized for.
Like whether you're optimized for likes or dislikes.
The ranking is just instead decided by those single centralized algorithms
controlled by those big companies.
And this concentration of power in these days has two consequences.
So first, you don't have no visibility into why certain content
appeal into their feed.
The algorithm is just a black box.
And we see a lot of complaints about them in these days.
And the second, creators often learn to optimize
for that particular algorithm to appease them.
And over time, we can see the content start to become homogeneous everywhere.
Like there are all kinds of clickbait kind of content.
And this is what Blue Sky does something different here.
Like they call algorithmic choice.
Blue Sky Custom Feeds allow users to create, subscribe, and share their feed algorithms
instead of being stuck with that particular default.
And on the consumer side, the field consumers, they can further subscribe to the feeds offered by their communities.
And on the creator side, third-party tools make it possible for anyone to build and publish their own feeds.
This connects to a very long-standing vision in the research community, the idea of middleware.
The core idea is simple.
What if the social media services, like recommendation, moderation, or the governance in the communities,
did not have to be controlled by the platforms?
What if they could be provided by these independent middleware services so that users have the choice among them?
And this is just a theoretical vision is already happening at scale.
Today, as people already know in the room, like the Blue Sky today has 42 million active users
and over 40,000 custom feeds have been created.
So this makes custom feeds as the first large-scale instantiation of the middleware vision
for feed recommendation specifically.
But for this particular ecosystem to work, someone has to actually build in their feeds.
And that's the feed creators we focus in this particular work.
But we know little about them.
Like, what motivates them to create feeds in the first place?
And what does it actually take to run a particular custom feed?
And what challenges and opportunities do they face?
These are the questions driving our work.
So in particular, we organize our work around these three research questions.
First, what kinds of custom feeds are there?
Second, what kinds of how do the feed creators building, maintaining, and sustaining their custom feed ecosystem?
Third, what kinds of challenges and opportunities exist for the custom feed ecosystem?
To answer these research questions, we conducted semi-structured interviews with 24 feed creators on Blue Sky.
And this particular chart shows the range of feeds they created, measured by the likes they received on Blue Sky.
Some creators that are relatively small, with under like 100 likes.
But there are others who are building feeds over 10,000 likes.
So we have a good spread in our interviews, from the hobbyist builders to some of the most popular feeds on the platform.
And here we talk about our findings.
Our first key finding is that we identify there are two kinds of feed orientations that the custom feeds can fall into.
The first is the utility-providing feed.
They are focused on delivering the content to the feed consumers, thinking of them as a well-curated feeder, filter.
And there's also community-building feeds that are very different.
They're cultivating the space for the feed consumers to connect with each other.
And as we will see, these two orientations shape their motivations, their daily tasks, and how they're sustaining their feeds in the long term.
Specific for the utility-providing feeds, the feed is primarily a content delivery mechanism for the feed consumers.
The creator's job is mostly done as setup.
They configure the hashtags, keywords, or data accounts, and just let the feed run on the blue sky.
On the other hand, the feed consumers, they are purely receptive or passive.
They just follow the feed like they're following a particular channel or follow a particular blog site.
They do not actively contribute to what particular content appears in the feed by themselves.
And within the utility feed, we also see two distinct subtypes.
The first is what we call set it and forget it mode.
People just catch the relevant post of a given topic just with a very simple list of keywords, such as mushrooms or some fan communities.
The creator just sets it up once and just largely forgot it and step away.
The second type is very highly curated feeds, those use vetted poster lists or custom ranking algorithms to ensure the content they delivered are super high quality.
It's almost like an editor's choice or editor's pick in the newspapers.
Unlike the first type, they require very ongoing maintenance.
The creators constantly turning and tuning and updating their algorithms to ensure the content is always high quality.
Community building feeds work very differently from these utility feeds.
Here, the feed consumers are not just passive recipients.

They actively contribute in the content and interact with each other simply by the feed, like they were adding a particular keyword to their post so that they're essentially having conversations with each other in the feed.
The feed becomes a very shared social place, and the feed creator's role shifts here too.
Instead of configuring rules, they tend to be social space, maintaining norms and keeping the community healthy.
I will give you two concrete examples of community building feeds.
So there's a particular feed that we found out in our interviews.

They just surface a post from the users below a particular threshold of follower numbers so that they can design it to welcome the newcomers and give the less known voices a chance to be seen in their feed.
And another, just like the feed consumers curating the content by themselves.
So they can just reply to any post on Blue Sky with a particular keyword.
And in this way, they are nominating the post to appear in the feed automatically.
And one of our participants captured the creator's role very well.
They said, I almost see myself as a librarian.
I'm making sure people are not yelling.
They are not picking up their trash and putting books back when they are supposed to be.
I'm making a place as presentable and welcoming as possible.
So they are just tending to that particular social space as a feed creator, which is very different from the utility feed creators.
And after that, let's take a look at how people are building their custom feeds.
And we found out that most feed creators navigate an ecosystem of third-party tools.
They do not have to start from scratch.
But for some of them, those tools are not enough for their needs.
So they end up building their own infrastructures.
So for example, most feed creators, they rely on third-party tools.
And there is a real diversity of options for them.
Brown Sky Feed, Blue Sky Feed creators, and Grace, just to name a few.
So the variety matters, it means that the creators can always choose a tool that best fits their needs and their technical levels.
Within these tools, creators try to combine various kinds of building blocks to shape what appears in the feed.
Some using those keywords or regular expression matching to catch a post on a specific topic.
Others try to maintain the vetted accounts list, hand-picking who got included in their feed.
And some also use a Blue Sky Labellers system to help filter the content as part of their content moderation efforts.
But the third-party do not work for everyone.
Some creators have the needs that go beyond what existing tools can support.
And they end up building their own infrastructures.
So for instance, one participant built a fully personalized recommendation system on their own.
Because they need a fine-grained interaction data that this third-party tool simply cannot access.
And another tried to run a custom implementation in Rust because their feed lodges are often too complex for any block-based tools offered by these third-party tools.
And they also need to support a much larger scale of use.
So in this way, these feed creators are not casual hobbyists who just try it for fun.
They are essentially running some independent technical infrastructure through the custom feeds.
And our third key finding is about what it takes to maintain a custom feed over time.
And it turns out the maintenance goes well beyond just keeping the hour with them running.
Creators are handling moderation, deriving discovery, and try to listen to the consumer feedback.
And for most of these tasks, they get very little support from the Blue Sky platform.
So for instance, the utility providing feed, the moderation effort is very different across these different kinds of feeds.
But for the utility providing feeds, it's more about largely upfront effort.
They set up some strict rules to deliver the content, which means that usually there isn't much toxic content to moderate for after the feed goes alive.
But for the community feed, it's often ongoing because the community itself is always evolving.
There's new member joins, there's new dynamic emerge, and norms need to be actively maintained.
And on top of that, the moderation deletion is also much more nuanced.
It's not just about the topic being misaligned with your feed topic, but instead it's a social deletion.
They need to carefully make sure their deletions do not hurt the community relationship.
And the Blue Sky also decentralizes the creation of the feed, but they do not decentralize the discovery of the feed.
The feed tab on the Blue Sky is often static, ranked by the popularities, serving the same set of feeds that were popular two years ago.
And for the new feeds, especially non-engaged ones, they have little chance to be seen by the newcomers.
And the creators need to take matters into their own time.
They have to promote the feeds by themselves, on the social media, in the communities, whenever they can.
And we heard these frustrations directed from the creators.
One participant told us,
I have been commenting on every single feed topic post I found, telling them about the feeds and how to set up their Blue Sky accounts.
And they've sent out thousands of messages.
And they concluded that, even though the feed is successful, but obviously it's not a sustainable strategy for a lot of feed creators.
And another participant complained that, if creating a new account and they go to the feed tab, you will see the same top feed from two years ago.
They are all in English and often only about the United States.
So it's not interesting for the people outside of the United States.
And the centralized algorithms can improve the continuously on these YouTube or TikTok platforms.
They're listening to your clicks, your skips, and the dwell times, and feedback into their recommendation algorithms.
But in the Blue Sky for the custom feeds, they have not that kind of infrastructure.
There are several kinds of feedback they can tap into, like they get some implicit feedbacks from the people who likes, replies, or reposts their feeds from their custom feeds.
But they lack the technical tools to actually analyze and learn from this data at scale.
They also got a lot of some sort of explicit feedback from their direct messages or some survey responses they distributed in their feed.
But it's often very rare and only come from the people who are mostly motivated to consume their feed content.
There are also some third-party tools that provide some basic analytics, things like the daily active users or the posted volumes.
But this only tells the feed creators that the feed is still alive, but not whether it's actually working on that.
The result is that the feed creators do not get a lot of feedback to continuously improving their feed.
And in the long term, they just lose their motivation to do that.
And our fourth and final key finding is about sustaining the custom feeds.
The custom feed ecosystem, over 40,000 feeds, run almost entirely on volunteer labels.
Creators need to adopt the cost of running the feed themselves, with very little to no platform support.
And what is sustaining them isn't their financial incentives or the platform recognitions.
It's often just intrinsic motivation.
And if we take a step back, there's some cost of sustaining a feed.
And on the infrastructure side, the creators need to pay out of the pocket for third-party tool subscriptions to keep their feed running.
And beyond the money, there's also time commitment.
They have to do the regular maintenance, moderation, and responding to the user feedback.
But what keeps the creator going, we call it the hobbyist model.
The motivation is the owning few.
For the utility-providing creators, seeing their subscriber account grow is a key signal.
It tells them the feed is still useful and worth maintaining.
But that also is saying that without that particular signal, feed creators are more likely to lose motivations.
And that's why we have this bunch of set and forget it feeds for the utility part.
For the community-building creators, the motivation comes from something very different.
They are seeing that people are actually connecting with each other through their feed.
And that makes them happy about their job.
But this mirrors what we see in those volunteer ecosystems like Reddit and Discord, where the community itself is just a reward.
We also asked about feed creators about monetarization, and most felt that not all the feeds can justify it.
The key factor is that whether the feed is built on dedicated, irreplaceable effort.
So, for example, the set and forget feeds, they are very easy to replicate.
If someone charges for it, users can just build the feeds by themselves, elsewhere.
But for the highly curated feeds, they are very different.
The creators have the ongoing effort and judgment to make the feed valuable to their feed consumers.
And we do see some successful examples of monetarization in that space in our interviews.
Creators volunteer their efforts very willingly, but they also think the infrastructure costs deserve some financial support.
One participant put it very simply that it's not about getting rich.
It's just about getting covering expense.
So I'm not going to pay out of the pocket to help the community.
And another friend, even more positive, they're charging a small amount of dollars isn't just fair to the creators.
It's creating a positive loop of dollars going through the communities so that the whole Blue Sky Custom Feeds can go more alive.
So with all these findings, we have these two design implications to see how we can further improve the Blue Sky Custom Feeds as a community,
and also as researchers.
We see there's two key directions to better support feed creators beyond the initial building process.
First, we need to help creators to better make sense of the implicit feedback they get from their feed creators.
Feed consumers like likes, reports, and replies exist.
But creators need to better choose to better turn into that noisy data,
into some actionable insights they can use to improve their feeds.
Second, we also need to rethink about the feed discovery.
Right now, it's still platform-controlled, even though it's very fair-controlled,
like it's always ranked by the popularities.
But enabling more personalized feed discovery, beyond just the platform-controlled ranking,
will give new users, and not English feeds, a real chance to reach to the right users.
And we also want to say something about the sustainability.
If creators burn out and cannot cover their costs, feeds just will stop being maintained,
and the communities building around them will slowly go away.
From our interviews, we've found two potential positives that the feed creators themselves are already experienced with.
One is their donations.
They will solicit some donations from their feed consumers.
This is similar to how the Wikipedia sustain itself through those volunteer-like community donations.
And we also see those popular feeds doing transparent advertising.
They have some ads within their feeds, but the ad is opt-in, and the community scoped ads,
so that they can keep the revenue, but do not deliver like very irrelevant ads to their consumers.
Although, neither of them is a perfect solution, but they both point out towards a model where the community of the custom feeds
can support the infrastructure and support itself in the long term.
Here's all of our presentations, and thank you for everyone listening, even though it's over Zoom.
And do you have any questions?
And here's my contact.
Do we have any questions, comments, thoughts?
Nope?
Oh, what you got?
If you were managing Blue Sky, the app, what would you do?
The question was, if you were managing Blue Sky, the app, what would you do?
I think, as I can see in the design implications, I would at least try to improve the feed discovery feature.
Like, I think people do want that part to be, like, it's inevitable they're going to be some sort of centralized
by the Blue Sky platform to design how to rank in these different kinds of feeds.
But obviously, I think one single feature you can see just to recommend relevant feeds for people to explore,
so that they're not being stuck in those popularity ranking feeds,
which is always just the same set of feeds all over the years.
And I think another important part people constantly want is about better guidelines,
about how to do the advertisement in their feeds.
Like, people are kind of trying to figure it out by themselves
and trying to testing that in their own population, the consumer's populations.
But it's more beneficial for them to see, like, what kinds of ads are tolerable in the feeds
so that they can be more actionable and more comfortable to place ads in their feeds
so that they're not going to do the free label for the whole ecosystem.
I think that's it. Thank you very much.
Yeah. Thank you, everyone.
Cheers.
